coredata.dc:title: Inhibitory effect of aptamer-carbon dot nanomaterial-siRNA complex on the metastasis of hepatocellular carcinoma cells by interfering with FMRP
coredata.prism:publicationName: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoliang
Yang, Jie
Zhang, jing
Wang, Xiangyun
Chen, Lulu
Zhang, Chao
Shen, Zhifa
coredata.dc:description: Using small interfering RNA (siRNA) for the specific gene-silencing has been a novel therapeutic method for the treatment of incurable diseases such as malignancies. However, it remains a challenge whether siRNA can be safely and effectively delivered into target cells. Therefore, we synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as a gene vector at the siRNA delivery system that induced efficient gene knockdown in vitro while binding aptamer AS1411 to resolve the difficulty in cell targeting. We found that CDs with adequate biocompatibility can improve the efficiency of cellular uptake of siRNA. CLSM and FCM results showed that CDs were mainly localized in the cytoplasm and emitted bright green fluorescence. In addition, the CD/siRNA delivery system mediated by the aptamer AS1411 effectively silenced the expression of Fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP) and successfully inhibited the migration and invasive propensity of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cells. In summary, we have synthesized a valuable siRNA delivery vector enabling not only bioimaging but also effective downregulation of gene expression, which is indicative of an efficient potential for gene delivery and therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hepatocellular carcinoma
Gene silence
Carbon dots
siRNA

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and Western Blot analysis
Synthesis and characterization of water-soluble amino carbon dots (CDs)
Binding efficiency of three kinds of CDs/nucleic acid complexes
Cytotoxic assessment of CDs complexes on human liver cells
Intracellular uptake studies of CDs complexes by HepG2 cells
The expression and characterization of NCL protein and FMRP
Efficient gene delivery and effective gene silencing by the CDs complexes
Inhibited migration, invasion, and colony formation of HepG2cell by CDs/AS1411/siRNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). (a) TEM images of CDs. (b) and (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (d) The fluorescence lifetime of CDs.

Agarose gel electrophoresis to evaluate the loading capacity of CDs. (a) Mixed solutions of CDs with FAM-AS1411 at various weight ratios. Lanes 2–7 correspond to CDs/FAM-AS1411 ratios of 2.5:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 40:1 and 80:1. Lanes 1 and 8 correspond to FAM-AS1411 and CDs. (b) Mixed solutions of CDs with siRNA at various weight ratios. Lanes 2–7 correspond to CDs/FAM-AS1411 ratios of 40:1, 80:1, 120:1, 160:1, 200:1 and 240:1. Lanes 1 and 8 correspond to siRNA and CDs. (c) and (D) Mixed solutions of CDs with FAM-AS1411 and siRNA at various weight ratios. Lanes 1–10 correspond to CDs/FAM-AS1411 ratios of 0:4:1, 320:4:1, 0:3:2, 320:3:2, 0:2.5:2.5, 320:2.5:2.5, 0:2:3, 320:2:3, 0:1:4, and 320:1:4. In Fig. (c) The mass of CDs in all proportions remained unchanged, while in Fig. (d) The mass of siRNA in all proportions remained unchanged.

Cell viability of liver cell lines treated with CDs. Viability of L02 cells treated with various concentrations of CDs for 24 h (a), 48 h (b), and 74 h (c). Viability of HepG2 cells treated with various concentrations of CDs for 24 h (d), 48 h (e), and 72 h(f).

Cellular uptake of the CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA. Cellular uptake of CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA after incubation with HepG2 cells, observed under different channels of the flow cytometer, (a)-FITC channel, (b)-Cy3 channel, and (c)-Cy5 channel. (d) Cellular uptake of CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA after incubation with HepG2 cells, observed by CLSM.

Expression of proteins in liver cell lines. (a) The expression of total NCL protein in liver cell lines. (b) The expression of membrane NCL protein in liver cell lines. (c) The expression of total FMRP in liver cell lines.
Gene silencing effect of siFmr1 delivered by different vectors. Gene silencing effect of siFmr1 delivered by (a) Lipofectamine 3000 reagent, (b) CDs/siRNA and (c) CDs/AS1411/siRNA.

The effect of CDs/AS1411/siRNA on the migration and invasion of HepG2 cells. (a) Migration, (b) invasion, (c) and colony formation of HepG2 cells after treatment with PBS, CD/AS1411/siNC, or CDs/AS1411/siRNA for 48 h.
Schematic illustration of the formation of CDs/AS1411/siRNA and the role in targeting cancer cell imaging and gene silencing.

head.ce:title: Inhibitory effect of aptamer-carbon dot nanomaterial-siRNA complex on the metastasis of hepatocellular carcinoma cells by interfering with FMRP

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Center for Molecular Oncology and Functional Nucleic Acids, School of Laboratory Medicine, Xinxiang Medical University, 601 Jinsui Avenue, Xinxiang 453003, Henan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Molecular Oncology and Functional Nucleic Acids

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using small interfering RNA (siRNA) for the specific gene-silencing has been a novel therapeutic method for the treatment of incurable diseases such as malignancies. However, it remains a challenge whether siRNA can be safely and effectively delivered into target cells. Therefore, we synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as a gene vector at the siRNA delivery system that induced efficient gene knockdown in vitro while binding aptamer AS1411 to resolve the difficulty in cell targeting. We found that CDs with adequate biocompatibility can improve the efficiency of cellular uptake of siRNA. CLSM and FCM results showed that CDs were mainly localized in the cytoplasm and emitted bright green fluorescence. In addition, the CD/siRNA delivery system mediated by the aptamer AS1411 effectively silenced the expression of Fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP) and successfully inhibited the migration and invasive propensity of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cells. In summary, we have synthesized a valuable siRNA delivery vector enabling not only bioimaging but also effective downregulation of gene expression, which is indicative of an efficient potential for gene delivery and therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is one of the most common and fatal malignant tumors whose morbidity and mortality are among the highest in the world [1,2]. It is challenging to diagnose early due to the inconspicuous symptoms and prone to extensive metastasis in the late phase, making treatment difficult. In 2020, there were about 910, 000 new cases and 830, 000 deaths of HCC worldwide, which seriously threaten human health.

FMRP, encoded by the FMR1 gene, is an RNA-binding protein that plays a vital role in regulating the translation of mRNA in postsynaptic neurons [3]. The low expression of FMRP can affect neuronal development and synaptic plasticity and lead to Fragile X syndrome (FXS) [4]. It was observed that FMRP is mainly a cytoplasmic protein with both nuclear localization and nuclear output signals, suggesting that FMRP has the potential to shuttle between cytoplasm and nucleus [5]. Although the related research on FMRP is mainly focused in the field of neuroscience, it is also widely expressed in a variety of tissues, with a high expression level in testicular tissues and a low expression level in normal liver tissues, indicating that this protein may also play a pivotal role in other organs or diseases [6–8]. Our previous study found that FMRP participates in the localization and translation of signal transducer and transcriptional activator 3 (STAT3) mRNA and mediates the metastasis of HCC. Based on the above reasons, we are expected to inhibit the metastasis of HCC cells by knocking down the expression of FMRP.

In recent years, gene therapy has become an emerging method to prevent and treat refractory diseases such as cancer [9,10]. RNA interference (RNAi) regulates mRNA stability and translation in almost all human cells [11]. siRNA molecules play a role in the process of RNAi, regulating the expression of target genes through a series of reaction processes such as RNA induction of silencing complex (RISC) and endogenous ribonuclease cleavage [12]. Thus, RNAi technology has become a highly innovative and valuable tool in gene therapy. However, RNAi technology still faces many challenges, such as the easy degradation of RNA molecules by enzymes in vitro and in vivo, the toxicity of transfection reagents, the low efficiency of transfection, etc. Therefore, an important process of RNA-mediated therapy is to select an appropriate gene delivery vector to protect nucleic acid molecules from degradation and complete the targeted delivery of siRNA [10,13].

At present, two major types of gene vectors have been developed: viral vectors (such as lentiviral vectors and adenovirus vectors) and non-viral vectors (such as cationic polymers and liposomes) [14,15]. Nevertheless, these carriers are not ideal gene delivery carriers due to their numerous drawbacks, such as stimulating the body to produce adverse immune responses, potent cytotoxicity to cells, inability to protect nucleic acids from the degradation of nucleases, and lack of targeting [16]. Based on the above reasons, we urgently hope to find a vector that is safe, non-toxic, easy to be metabolized by the body and can efficiently deliver genes to the disease site for targeted gene therapy. In recent years, with the increasingly close connection between nanotechnology and the medical industry, significant progress has been made in the treatment of combining nanomaterials with medicine [17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), referred to as carbon dots (CDs), an incidentally discovered carbon nanomaterial with a very small size (generally 1–10 nm), has attracted considerable interest from researchers due to its low toxicity, satisfactory biocompatibility, easy synthesis and modification, and photoluminescence properties [18,19]. So far, CDs with various synthetic methods have been widely used in sensors, photocatalysis, biological imaging, and other fields [17,20,21]. The study showed that the introduction of CDs could also enhance the stability of nucleic acid molecules and promote their cellular internalization [22]. In a word, based on the excellent characteristics of CDs, it can be seen as a promising vector for gene or drug delivery.

The aptamer is a single-stranded DNA or RNA that can bind to targets with high specificity and affinity through screening from a random library by using the exponentially enriched ligand phylogenetic technique (SELEX), which is considered as one of the candidates for early diagnosis and targeted therapy of tumors with several features of a wide range of targets, lower immunogenicity and easier to modify compared with traditional antibodies [23,24]. Therefore, aptamer-mediated RNAi processes can be used for gene silencing and targeted gene therapy [25]. It is well known that AS1411 is a common guanine-rich DNA aptamer composed of 26 oligonucleotides, which can bind specifically to certain tumor cells highly expressing nucleolin protein (NCL) [26]. NCL is a multifunctional phosphorylated protein that participates in many cellular processes, including transcription, packaging, and transport of rRNA, as well as DNA replication and recombination [22,27]. It has been found that NCL is highly expressed in various malignant tumors such as gastrointestinal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, human breast cancer, and glioblastoma [28–31]. Hence, AS1411 was utilized for specifically identifying HepG2 cells.

Herein, water-soluble amino CDs linked with aptamer were used as a gene-targeted delivery vector to silence the expression of specific genes and explored its effect on the metastatic ability of HepG2 cells. Scheme 1 describes the formation of the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex and their use for cell imaging and gene delivery against target cells. First, the size and fluorescence properties of the synthesized CDs were characterized. Secondly, we assessed the cytotoxicity of CDs and loaded AS1411 and siRNA on the surface of CDs through electrostatic interactions. Next, we evaluated the cell imaging efficacy and cell uptake of the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex by FCM and CLSM experiments. Finally, the gene silencing effects of CDs/AS1411/siRNA and lipo3000/siRNA were compared and analyzed. CDs/AS1411/siRNA can effectively inhibit the metastasis of HCC cells.

Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 (RPMI-1640) were purchased from Gibco (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Lonsera (UY). Lipofectamine 3000 (Lipo 3000) was purchased from Invitrogen. Amine water-soluble fluorescent CDs were purchased from Janus (China), whereas Cy5-labeled siRNAs (siRNA target sequence: AAGCTAGAAGCTTTCTCGAAT) were obtained from RIBOBIO (China) and Cy3-AS1411 (AS1411sequence:5′-GGTGGTGGTGGTTGTGGTGGTGGTGG-3′) was purchased from TSINGKE (China).

The morphology of the CDs solution was observed by transmission electron microscopy (Nikon, Japan). First, the CDs solution was added to a 96-wellplate, and then the ultraviolet absorption spectra were determined using a microplate reader. Next, fluorescence spectra of CDs were analyzed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan) under different light excitations (400–490 nm). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was provided by Janus Company.

To explore the binding effect of CDs with different nucleic acids, we mixed CDs with AS1411 in different mass concentration ratios of 2.5:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 40:1, and 80:1 while combining CDs with siFmr1 in various mass concentration ratios of 40:1, 80:1, 120:1, 160:1, 200:1, and 240:1. We then obtained the optimal binding ratio through an agarose gel retardation experiment. All the samples were mixed with 6 × Loading buffer and loaded on a 2% agarose gel. After running the gel at 80 V for 15 min, the gel was imaged with a gel imager (Bio-Rad). Similarly, we mixed CDs with AS1411 and siRNA in proportions of 320:4:1, 320:3:2, 320:2.5:2.5, 320:2:3, and 320:1:4 to obtain their optimal binding ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and Western Blot analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of HCC cells, HepG2, and MHCC97H cells, were maintained in DMEM with 10% FBS, while the human normal liver cell-L02 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 with 10% FBS. All of them were incubated at 37 °C in an incubator with 5% CO2. All three cells were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Sciences. For western blot assay, the three kinds of cells were washed with PBS 3 times and subsequently added into RIPA cell lysate (Beyotime, China) for lysis on ice for 5 min. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 4 °C to obtain the total protein, and the protein concentration was measured using the Bicinchoninic Acid Assay (BCA) kit (Beyotime, China). Next, proteins from different cells were separated by 8% SDS-PAGE gel and transferred to a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Millipore Corp, USA). The membranes were blocked with 5% skimmed milk at room temperature for 2 h and then incubated at 4 °C overnight with the following primary antibodies: Nucleolin (1:1000, Abcam), FMRP (1:1000, Abcam), and Tubulin (1:1000, Proteintech). After washing all strips with Tris-buffered saline-Tween (TBST) 3 times, they were incubated in HRP-conjugated corresponding secondary antibodies (1:1000) at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, the protein on the strips was visualized with an enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) system. The expression levels of relative proteins were quantified and analyzed by the Image J software.

We measured the cytotoxicity of CDs using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) (Dojindo Laboratories, Japan). At first, 10 mg/ml CDs stock solution was diluted into different concentrations of CDs solution. The cells (L02/HepG2) were plated in 96-well plates with roughly 5 × 103 cells per well and cultured for an appropriate time at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h, respectively. Next, 10 µL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well and incubated for approximately 3 h. Finally, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader.

Approximately 1 × 105 HepG2 cells were seeded into 6-well plates cultured for 24 h. Then, the CD/AS1411/siFmr1 complex with an optimal weight ratio was added to every well. The Lipofectamine 3000 Reagent/siFmr1 complex was used as the control group. After incubation for 48 h, the culture medium was discarded, and the HepG2 cells were rinsed with PBS thrice. Finally, all the cells were collected by centrifugation after adding cell lysate, proteins were extracted, and the protein expression level was verified via Western Blot assay.

Flow cytometry (FCM) was used to evaluate the transfection efficiency of the CDs/Cy3-AS1411, CDs/Cy5-siRNA and CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA complexes. About 2 × 105 HepG2 cells were pre-plated in 6-well plates and cultured at 37 °C with 5% CO2 until cell fusion was approximately 30%, after which CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex (0.4 nmol for siRNA) in the optimal weight ratio (320:4:1) was added to each well and subsequently cultured for 24 h, and then all the cells were washed with PBS 3 times by centrifugation. Finally, all the cells were resuspended in 400 μL PBS and passed through nylon mesh with 300 meshes to acquire a single-cell suspension used for the flow cytometry assay.

HepG2 cells were seeded in 24-well plates and cultured for 24 h. The Cy3 group was modified at the 5 'end of the aptamer AS1411 (Cy3-AS1411), whereas the Cy5 group was modified at the 5′ end of siRNA (Cy5-siRNA). The Cy3-AS1411 and Cy5-siRNA were combined with CDs by electrostatic bonding. The CDs/Cy3-AS1411, CDs/Cy5-siRNA and CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA complexes with an optimal weight ratio were incubated with HepG2 cells for a specific time under identical conditions. After washing with PBST 3 times, all the samples were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and stained with DAPI for 10 min in the dark. The samples were rewashed and Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) was utilized to visualize cellular imaging、cellular uptake, and localization of these three complexes.

A wound-healing assay was utilized for measuring the HepG2 cell migration ability. First, about 1 × 105 cells were pre-seeded into 6-well plates, and CDs/AS1411/siRNA complexes with an optimal weight ratio were added and incubated for 24 h. Then three scratches were made on the cell layer with a 200 µL pipette tip. Subsequently, cells were washed three times with PBS, cultured in DMEM, and photos were taken at 0 h. Afterward, the medium was changed every 24 h, and changes in the scratch were photographed. Finally, 0-hour and 48-hour scratch healing areas were selected to calculate cell mobility.

We carried out the cell migration and invasion experiment in 24-well transwell chambers (Corning, USA, 8-μm) to assess the invasion and migration ability of HepG2 cells. HepG2 cells were pre-plated in 6-well plates, then CDs/AS1411/siRNA complexes were added to every plate and subsequently incubated for 24 h. PBS and CDs/AS1411/siNC were chosen as blank and normal control, respectively. After being washed with PBS 3 times, the cells were digested and centrifuged. Next, 2.0 × 105 cells were seeded into the upper chamber with 200 µL cellsuspension, and a 700 µL culture medium containing 18% FBS was added to the lower chamber and cultivated at a constant temperature incubator for 48 h. The medium was disposed of, and the cells were washed with PBS. Afterward, all the cells were fixed with 600 µL 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min and stained with 600 µL 0.1% crystal violet for 15 min. Finally, all the cells in the upper chamber were gently wiped away, and five visual fields (×100) were randomly selected under the microscope for observation. All the steps of the invasion experiment were similar to that of migration, except that in the first step, the matrix gel was diluted 40 times by DMEM and added into the chamber for more than 2 h at 37 °C.

HepG2 cells were incubated with PBS, CDs/AS1411/siNC and CDs/AS1411/siFmr1 for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Subsequently, 1 × 103 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and allowed to grow for about two weeks and with the medium changed every two or three days. After that, colonies were fixed with 1 mL methanol for 30 min and stained with 1 mL crystal violet staining for 20 min. The number and size of every colony were observed and counted, and the results were statistically analyzed.

In this study, T-test and One-way analysis of variance test were used for data analysis, statistical significance was performed using GraphPad Prism 9.0 software, and P values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. ****P < 0.0001, ***P < 0.001, **<0.01, *P < 0.05, while N. S. stands for P > 0.05. All data were obtained from at least three independent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of water-soluble amino carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, water-soluble amino CDs were synthesized by the Nanjing Janus Company. The morphology and structure of CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs were spherical and slightly agglomerated, with a diameter of <10 nm. CDs show strong absorption in the ultraviolet region because they can effectively capture electrons under short-wavelength ultraviolet light. The result of the multimode platereader illustrated that the UV absorption peak of CDs was at 366 nm. The aqueous solution of CDs was light brown under white light, whereas it emitted strong blue-green fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1b). The results of the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer exhibited that the emission wavelength of CDs was constantly varied with the change of excitation wavelength. CDs had the maximum emission wavelength when the excitation wavelength was 440 nm, and the peak value was around 540 nm (Fig. 1c). The fluorescence lifetime refers to the time required for the fluorescence intensity of the substance to decrease to 1/e of the maximum fluorescence intensity generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding efficiency of three kinds of CDs/nucleic acid complexes
ce:section.ce:para: Agarose gel electrophoresis was used to evaluate the optimal binding ratio of CDs with AS1411 or siRNA. We combined CDs and FAM-AS1411 (AS1411 was modified with a fluorescent group at its 5′ end to facilitate subsequent gel imaging analysis) with various mass concentration ratios of 2.5:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 40:1, 80:1 and verified by agarose gel electrophoresis. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the band brightness of AS1411 gradually decreased with an increase of CDs, indicating that the combination of CDs and AS1411 gradually increased. When the ratio of CDs/AS1411 reached 40:1, the AS1411 band almost disappeared, proving that the AS1411 was fully bound to CDs. In addition, we chose the 5:1 and 40:1 ratios to make CDs and AS1411 to react at 4 °C, 25 °C, and room temperature, respectively, to exclude the influence of temperature on the combination of the two. No obvious difference was observed in the brightness of the bands at different temperatures. Therefore, room temperature can be used as a further experimental condition (Fig. s1). Then, we mixed CDs with siRNA at the mass to mass concentration ratios of 40:1, 80:1, 120:1, 160:1, 200:1, and 240:1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, the siRNA bands were almost invisible when the ratio was equal to or above 160:1, indicating that the siRNA was fully bound to CDs. To obtain the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex, three materials were mixed according to the ratios of 320:4:1, 320:3:2, 320:2.5:2.5, 320:2:3 and 320:1:4, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c, when CDs were not added, AS1411 and siRNA could not conjugate with each other, there was no electrostatic interaction, and their lengths were similar. Therefore, the bands of AS1411 and siRNA overlapped, and the mobility was relatively fast. When CDs were added to the mixture of AS1411 and siRNA in different weight proportions, the mobility of the band decreased significantly due to electrostatic interaction. By observing the band brightness changes of the substrate (namely AS1411 and siRNA) before and after the addition of CDs, it was observed that when the ratio of the three was 320:4:1, the binding effect was highest; similarly, keeping the amount of siRNA in all ratios unchanged, the assembly efficiency of the three complexes was confirmed by changing the weight of CDs and AS1411. According to the analysis of Fig. 2d, the optimal binding ratio of the three was still 320:4:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic assessment of CDs complexes on human liver cells
ce:section.ce:para: We used the CCK-8 assay to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs on normal human liver cells L02 and neoplastic liver cells HepG2. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . When cultured for 24–48 h, there was only slight toxicity to L02 and HepG2 even if the concentration of CDs reached 1000 μg/ml, and the survival rate of all cells was above 90%. Even after the incubation time was extended to 72 h, the cell activity remained above 85% when the CDs concentration was <600 μg/mL. However, if the concentration of CDs was more than 600 μg/ml, the cell activity remained above 75%, causing mild cytotoxicity. Based on the above experiments, in the concentration range (<600 μg/mL) and culture time (≤48 h) of CDs used later in the experiment, we can conclude that the toxicity of CDs to cells is negligible, and it possesses adequate biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular uptake studies of CDs complexes by HepG2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: We used Cy3 modified AS1411 (Cy3-AS1411) and Cy5 modified siRNA (Cy5-siRNA) to couple with CDs, and the intracellular uptake of CDs/AS1411, CDs/siRNA and CDs/AS1411/siRNA complexes by HepG2 cells was monitored by CLSM and FCM. As shown in Fig. s2: compared with the control group, the fluorescence peak of the cells incubated with CDs/Cy3-AS1411 shifted to the right under FITC and Cy3 channels, which means the complex can be absorbed into HepG2 cells. There was no early extracellular degradation of complex. Meanwhile, CLSM results revealed that prominent green (CDs), red (AS1411), and purple (siRNA) fluorescence could be simultaneously observed in HepG2. Furthermore, fluorescence did not vary significantly over time, proving that cells easily absorbed the complex and remained stable and difficult to degrade in the cells (Fig. s3). These results indicate that HepG2 cells can effectively absorb CDs/Cy3-AS1411. Similarly, after HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs/Cy5-siRNA, FCM results exhibited pronounced fluorescence peak shifts to the right under FITC and Cy5 channels while CLSM simultaneously observed strong green (CDs) and purple (siRNA) fluorescence around the nucleus. These results indicate that HepG2 cells can effectively absorb CDs/Cy5-siRNA. Finally, FCM results showed that the peak values of the three fluorescence channels (FITC, Cy3, and Cy5) shifted to the right compared with the control group after HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs/Cy3-AS1411/Cy5-siRNA, demonstrating that the complex successfully entered HepG2 cells (Fig. 4 a-c). CLSM also obtained similar results (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The expression and characterization of NCL protein and FMRP
ce:section.ce:para: Nucleolin is a ubiquitous molecule with multiple functions, including ligand binding and traveling between cell surfaces, cytoplasm, and nucleus [32]. It has been reported that AS1411 could bind to NCL protein on the surface of tumor cells [22]. Therefore, we verified the expression levels of total NCL protein and membrane NCL protein in L02, HepG2, and MHCC97H by WB. It was found in Fig. 5 a that the expression of total NCL protein in HepG2 cells was substantially higher than that of L02 cells (P < 0.001) and MHCC97H cells (P < 0.01). Indeed, the expression trend of membrane NCL protein was consistent with that of total NCL. FMRP is encoded by the FMR1 gene. Related studies have shown that FMRP is closely related to the occurrence and development of liver cancer [33,34]. Our previous study found that FMRP can bind to STAT3 mRNA and promote the formation of liver cancer metastasis. Therefore, we extracted the total protein of three kinds of cells to explore the expression of FMRP in L02, HepG2 and MHCC97H by Western Blot. As shown in Fig. 5b, the expression level of FMRP in HepG2 cells was significantly higher than that in L02 cells (P < 0.001) and MHCC97H cells (P < 0.001). Thus, it was proved that FMRP was highly expressed in HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient gene delivery and effective gene silencing by the CDs complexes
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the gene silencing efficiency of siRNA (siFmr1), we delivered siRNA to HepG2 by transfection reagent Lipofectamine 3000, CDs/siRNA (160:1), and CDs/AS1411/siRNA (320:4:1) complexes and analyzed the knockdown efficiency by Western Blot. As shown in Fig. 6 a, when siRNA was transfected with Lipo3000 reagent, the expression level of FMRP in the experimental group knockdown by siFmr1 was significantly lower when compared to the control group (siNC, disordered siRNA), which proves that synthetic siFmr1 could effectively silence the expression level of FMRP, and the difference was statistically significant (P < 0.001). It can be seen from Fig. 6b that when siRNA is delivered by the CDs/siRNA (160:1) complex, if the amount of siRNA used is the same as that of the Lipo3000 transfection method (0.4 nmol), the expression level of FMRP in the experimental group was also significantly decreased compared with the control group, indicating that the CDs/siRNA complex can also effectively deliver siRNA to HepG2 cells and effectively inhibit the expression of FMRP (P < 0.01). It was observed in Fig. 6c that when siRNA was 0.2 nmol and 0.4 nmol, the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex could both effectively deliver genes and knockdown FMRP, and the knockout efficiency of the complex was about 40% (P < 0.01) and 80% (P < 0.001), respectively. The transfection effect was not only the same or even higher than that of Lipo 3000 transfected gene but also better than that of CDs/ siRNA (160:1) complex without aptamer. These results suggest that CDs/AS1411/siRNA can stably transfer genes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibited migration, invasion, and colony formation of HepG2cell by CDs/AS1411/siRNA
ce:section.ce:para: To further analyze the effect of CDs/AS1411/siRNA on the migration and invasion of HepG2 cells, we carried out a series of experiments after silencing FMRP in HepG2 cells with the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex. The results of the wound healing experiment are shown in Fig. 7 a. Compared with the blank control treatment and CDs/AS1411/siNC group, the healing area of cells in the CDs/AS1411/siRNA group significantly decreased after 48 h, indicating that the complex could effectively inhibit the migration of HepG2 cells. Moreover, we conducted transwell assays after the knockdown of FMRP, and the CDs/AS1411/siRNA group observed a reduction in the number of cells passing through the transwell chamber (Fig. 7b). Based on the above results, we concluded that compared with the blank group and CDs/AS1411/siNC,CDs/AS1411/siRNA effectively inhibited the migration and invasion of HepG2 cells. Subsequently, statistical analysis of the number and size of colonies formed in each treatment group showed that the colony formation of HepG2 cells induced by CDs/AS1411/siRNA was lower than that of the blank control group and CDs/AS1411/siNC, proving that CDs/AS1411/siRNA could inhibit colony formation of HepG2 cells（Fig. 7c）. In conclusion, CDs/AS1411/siRNA can effectively silence the expression of FMRP and inhibit the metastasis of HCC cells.

In this work, amino water-soluble carbon dots were synthesized from glucose and TEPA. The positively charged CDs could carry nucleic acids to deliver genes. At the same time, it can emit bright green fluorescence and can be used for biological imaging. The combination of CDs with AS1411 and siRNA by electrostatic interaction can facilitate the targeted delivery of siRNA to HepG2 cells with higher efficiency. Furthermore, the CDs/AS1411/siRNA complex with high cellular uptake ability can effectively silence gene expression and inhibit the metastasis of HCC cells compared with the control. Combining CDs as a gene carrier with DNA/siRNA not only is a more environmentally friendly synthetic method than traditional methods but can produce a complex with high selectivity and specificity for tumor cells. Thus, the application and mechanism of these complexes in tumor therapy are worthy of further study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinxiang Medical University (grant number 505143) and the Key Scientific Research Project of Higher Education Institutions of Henan Province (grant number 14A180035).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Forner, M. Reig, J. Bruix, Hepatocellular carcinoma, The Lancet, 391 (2018) 1301-1314.
A. Villanueva, Hepatocellular Carcinoma, N Engl J Med, 380 (2019) 1450-1462.
